risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Randomisation is at the patient level and is performed by using an online randomisation system set up by the King’s Clinical Trials Unit (KCTU)... Randomisation is stratified by treatment centre. A set of patients is randomly assigned with fixed block sizes of two..." Comment: This describes a centralized online randomization system with stratification and blocked allocation, which is consistent with a proper random sequence generation process. Although the exact algorithm is not named, the reported method provides sufficient context to judge the allocation sequence as truly random.

Quote: "Randomisation is at the patient level and is performed by using an online randomisation system set up by the King’s Clinical Trials Unit (KCTU)... The system immediately notifies the trial coordinator of successful randomisation and the trial arm to which the participant has been allocated." Comment: An online central randomisation system controlled by the trials unit is an explicit concealment mechanism that prevents the enrolling researcher from foreseeing the next assignment before enrollment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Participants and carers also invited" ... "The course was delivered by an epilepsy nurse specialist and an EEG technician for 16 h over 2 consecutive days." and "At the start of each follow-up assessment, interview participants are requested not to reveal to the researcher which group they were allocated to." Comment: This was an educational self-management course versus usual care, so participants and delivering personnel could not realistically be blinded to allocation. Because the intervention could plausibly affect subjective outcomes such as quality of life, self-management, seizure reporting, and psychological distress, lack of blinding creates a likely risk of performance bias across the study outcomes overall. Although outcome assessors were blinded, that does not address this domain.

Quote: "Outcome assessors and the trial statistician will be blind to participants’ allocated treatment arm. At the start of each follow-up assessment, interview participants are requested not to reveal to the researcher which group they were allocated to. If a researcher is unblinded at the 6-month outcome visit, a different researcher will conduct the next follow-up assessment." Comment: The study explicitly states that outcome assessors were blinded, and procedures were in place to handle accidental unblinding. The main outcomes included questionnaire-based and interview-based measures, which could be influenced by assessor knowledge, so explicit blinding is relevant and supports a low risk judgement.

Summary: The study reports that at 12 months 81.9% of participants remained in the study, with follow-up slightly lower in the intervention group (79.5%, n=163) than in TAU (84.4%, n=168). The protocol also states that attrition was anticipated and accounted for in the sample size, and that efforts such as telephone reminders and vouchers were used to maximise follow-up. Comment: Overall attrition is reasonable and only modestly imbalanced, with no clear signal that missingness was related to the outcome; this supports Low risk for attrition bias.
